findings.description: The original PDF uses an annotated diagram layout with a central tag image and labeled arrows pointing to different parts of the tag, with explanatory text on both sides. The HTML converts this into a simple two-column table layout, losing the visual diagram/annotation structure.
findings.evidence: PDF shows a shellstock tag as a central visual element with arrows and callout annotations on left and right sides. HTML renders this as a flat table with 'Tag Content' and 'Requirement / Annotation' columns.

findings.description: The central shellstock tag diagram/image with arrows and annotations is completely missing from the HTML. The PDF presents a visual example of a sale tag with border and formatting, which is absent in the HTML.
findings.evidence: PDF shows a bordered 'SALE TAG' example with formatted content inside a box shape with annotation arrows. HTML has no image or diagram, only text in table cells.

findings.description: The 'KEEP REFRIGERATED' text in the PDF is displayed in a very large, bold font to emphasize its importance. In the HTML it is rendered in the same bold style as other labels, losing the visual emphasis of the larger font size.
findings.evidence: PDF: 'KEEP REFRIGERATED' appears in significantly larger bold font. HTML: appears as regular bold text similar to other headings.

findings.description: The left-side annotations in the PDF (Harvest Date description, Harvest Area description, 90 Day tag retention notice, Notice that product must remain refrigerated, Consumer Advisory Notice) are presented alongside the tag. In the HTML, these annotations are merged into the right column as 'Requirement/Annotation', changing the spatial reading order from a two-sided annotation to a single right-column annotation.
findings.evidence: PDF has annotations on both left and right sides of the central tag. HTML consolidates all annotations to a single right column.

findings.description: The PDF does not contain a traditional data table. The content is a visual example of a shellstock tag with annotations. The HTML converts this into a two-column table (Tag Content / Requirement / Annotation), which is a reasonable structural representation but not a faithful reproduction of the original layout — the PDF shows a single tag image/diagram with callout annotations pointing to different parts. This is an acceptable reformatting choice rather than a data integrity issue.
findings.evidence: PDF shows a single tag example with arrows and margin annotations; HTML presents this as a 9-row table with 'Tag Content' and 'Requirement / Annotation' columns.

findings.description: The document has only one heading (h1) for the title, which is correct. However, the PDF doesn't have a clear sub-heading hierarchy, so the flat structure is acceptable. No issues with heading hierarchy.
findings.evidence: Single h1: 'SHELLSTOCK TAG INFORMATION REQUIREMENTS' — matches PDF title.
